1: The temporal marker — the word came to Jeremiah in the tenth year of Zedekiah, which was the eighteenth year of Nebuchadnezzar — situates this oracle at the lowest point of the Babylonian siege. YHWH speaks his most hopeful message precisely when the situation is most desperate. The NT pattern matches: the gospel's most radical proclamation of hope (resurrection, eternal life) is delivered from the cross — the moment of maximum apparent defeat. The word of God comes not when circumstances are favorable but in defiance of circumstances.
2: The army of Babylon besieging Jerusalem while Jeremiah the prophet was shut up in the court of the guard — the prophet imprisoned while the enemy advances — is the pattern of truth-telling under persecution. Paul writes his most hopeful letters from prison (Philippians, Ephesians, Colossians); the persecuted community of Hebrews 11 acts on faith in things not seen. The imprisoned prophet prefigures the apostolic witness: "the word of God is not bound" (2 Timothy 2:9) even when its messengers are.
3: Zedekiah's charge that Jeremiah prophesied: YHWH will give this city into the hand of the king of Babylon — the accusation that the prophet's truth is treasonous. Jesus faces the same accusation: the religious authorities charge that his message undermines the nation's survival (John 11:50: "it is better for one man to die for the people than for the whole nation to perish"). The prophetic word that tells unpleasant truth is always accused of disloyalty; the NT resolves the tension by showing that the one who speaks truth is the one who saves, not destroys.
4: The prophecy that Zedekiah will be given into the hand of the king of Babylon and speak face to face with him — the covenant king handed over to the pagan king — is the judgment on unfaithful leadership. The NT has its counterpart: Jesus is handed over by the religious authorities to the Roman governor (Matthew 27:2); Pilate speaks with him face to face. But where Zedekiah was taken captive, Jesus's "handover" is the voluntary self-giving of the Son to accomplish redemption (John 10:17-18; Galatians 2:20).
5: Zedekiah taken to Babylon until YHWH attends to him — the waiting in captivity for divine visitation. The phrase "until YHWH attends to him" (ʿad pqodd oto YHWH) is the language of divine visitation/attention. Luke 1:68 uses the same concept: "Blessed be the Lord God of Israel, for he has visited and redeemed his people." The divine "visiting" (epeskepsato) that Zechariah celebrates in the Benedictus is the fulfillment of every "until YHWH attends to" in the OT prophets. Christ's coming is the great divine visitation.
6: The word of YHWH coming to Jeremiah — Hanamel son of your uncle Shallum will come to you — is a prophetic foreknowledge that establishes the sign-act as YHWH-directed. The foreknowledge of the prophets is a type of the omniscience Christ exercises: "Jesus, knowing that the Father had given all things into his hands" (John 13:3); he foretells Judas's betrayal (John 13:21-27), Peter's denial (John 13:38), and the destruction of the temple (Matthew 24:2). The prophet's foreknowledge is a partial and delegated form of the divine omniscience fully expressed in Christ.
7: The right of redemption and purchase belonging to Jeremiah — the kinsman-redeemer obligation (gaʾulah) — is the legal mechanism that drives the sign-act. The goʾel (kinsman-redeemer) is one of the richest OT types of Christ: Boaz redeeming Ruth (Ruth 4:1-12), the goʾel of Job 19:25 ("I know that my Redeemer lives"). Christ is the kinsman-redeemer of humanity — becoming flesh to stand in kinship with humanity and exercise the right of redemption on behalf of those who had forfeited their inheritance through sin (Galatians 4:4-5; Hebrews 2:14-17).
8: Hanamel's arrival confirming the word — then I knew that this was the word of YHWH — establishes the sign-act as genuine prophetic action. The prophetic word fulfilled in historical event is the pattern of biblical revelation. The NT presents Jesus's fulfillment of OT prophecy as the authentication of his messianic identity: Matthew's formula "this was to fulfill what was spoken by the prophet" applies this verification logic repeatedly (Matthew 1:22; Jeremiah 2:17; Jeremiah 4:14; etc.). Historical fulfillment of prophetic word is the mark of genuine revelation.
9: Jeremiah weighing seventeen shekels of silver for the field — the precise financial transaction documented in a crisis context — is the detail that makes the sign-act concrete. The purchase of the field with silver during a siege is the enacted parable of faith in future restoration when present evidence points only to loss. The thirty pieces of silver for which Judas sold Jesus (Matthew 26:15) — echoing Zechariah 11:12-13 — is a dark mirror of this field-purchase: where Jeremiah's silver secured a hopeful future, Judas's silver secured the arrest of the one who would accomplish the true restoration.
10: The deed of purchase signed and sealed and witnessed — the full legal formality of an irrevocable property transaction — is the sign of covenant seriousness. The NT uses the legal-seal image for the Spirit: "you were sealed with the promised Holy Spirit, who is the guarantee of our inheritance" (Ephesians 1:13-14). The sealed deed of Jeremiah's field purchase is a type of the Spirit as the seal and down-payment on the eschatological inheritance — the legal guarantee of the promised land that cannot be voided.
11: The two copies of the deed — the sealed copy containing the terms and the open copy — the sealed and the open portions of the ancient land deed, preserved as legal documentation. The image of a sealed and opened document recurs in Revelation 5:1-9: the seven-sealed scroll that only the Lamb is worthy to open — the title deed of creation itself, held by the one who redeems what was lost. Jeremiah's sealed deed of his field purchase is a type of Christ's claim to and redemption of the whole creation.
12: The deed given to Baruch son of Neriah in the presence of witnesses — Jeremiah's personal secretary receiving the legal document. Baruch is the faithful scribe who serves as the prophetic word's custodian. The NT parallel is the apostolic community as scribes and witnesses: "we are witnesses of all that he did" (Acts 10:39); "what we have seen and heard we proclaim to you" (1 John 1:3). The faithful preservation and transmission of the prophetic word is the task of the community of witnesses.
13: The command to Baruch before all who witnessed the signing: take these deeds, both the sealed and the open, and put them in an earthen vessel, that they may last for a long time — the preservation of the documents for future generations. The earthen vessel that preserves the documents of hope is the type Paul uses in 2 Corinthians 4:7: "we have this treasure in jars of clay, to show that the surpassing power belongs to God and not to us." The gospel of the new covenant, like the deed of Jeremiah's field, is carried in earthen vessels and preserved across time for those who will inherit the promise.
14: The purpose of the preservation — that they may last for a long time — is faith directed at a future generation. Hebrews 11:13: "these all died in faith, not having received the things promised, but having seen them and greeted them from afar." The deeds sealed and stored for future use are the structure of prophetic faith: acting now on a promise whose fulfillment lies beyond the current actor's lifetime. The church, bearing the promises of the new covenant, lives in the same structure — possessing the deeds but awaiting the full possession of the inheritance.
15: The sign-act's declared meaning — houses and fields and vineyards shall again be bought in this land — is the promise of restored normal life after the catastrophe. The resurrection of normal human life under divine blessing is the eschatological hope the NT presents in creation-renewal terms: Romans 8:21 ("the creation itself will be set free from its bondage to corruption and obtain the freedom of the glory of the children of God"). The future buying of fields and vineyards is the concrete OT expression of what Paul calls the "restoration of all things" (Acts 3:21) and Revelation calls the "new earth" (Revelation 21:1).
16: Jeremiah's prayer after the transaction — after I had given the deed to Baruch, I prayed to YHWH — shows the prophet immediately turning the sign-act into prayer. The pattern of act-then-prayer mirrors Jesus's own practice: after feeding the five thousand (John 6:11-15), after healing (Luke 5:16), before the cross (John 17). The prophetic sign-act is not complete until the human agent brings it before YHWH in prayer — the external act and the internal communion belong together.
17: The opening of the prayer — Ah, Lord YHWH! It is you who have made the heavens and the earth by your great power and by your outstretched arm. Nothing is too hard for you — grounds the prophetic hope in creation-theology. The "nothing too hard" (lo yipalleʾ davar) echoes Genesis 18:14 (God's word to Sarah about Isaac: "Is anything too hard for YHWH?"). Luke 1:37 applies the same logic to the virgin birth: "nothing will be impossible with God." The omnipotence of the creator God is the logical ground for miraculous intervention — applied by Gabriel to the incarnation as by YHWH to Sarah's pregnancy.
18: The prayer's recollection of YHWH showing steadfast love to thousands, but repaying the guilt of fathers to their children after them — the covenant formula of Exodus 20:5-6, here cited in context of intercession. Jesus does not quote this retributive half of the covenant formula; he instead emphasizes the mercy ("seventy times seven", Matthew 18:22). But the NT does not abandon the covenant justice — rather, Christ's cross is where the full weight of covenant guilt is absorbed, so that the "visiting iniquity" falls on the Son rather than on later generations (2 Corinthians 5:21; Galatians 3:13).
19: YHWH's great counsel and mighty works, whose eyes are open to all the ways of all mankind — divine omniscience applied to history. The "great counsel" (gadol haʿetsah) is the divine plan that encompasses all of history. Ephesians 1:11 describes this as the "plan of him who works all things according to the counsel of his will." The "mystery of his will" that Paul reveals is Christ as the center of all history — the one in whom the "great counsel" of Jeremiah's prayer is focused and fulfilled (Ephesians 1:9-10).
20: The recollection of signs and wonders in Egypt and to this day, in Israel and among all mankind — the Exodus miracles as the paradigm of divine saving power. Peter's Pentecost sermon (Acts 2:22) applies the same paradigm to Jesus: "a man attested to you by God with mighty works and wonders and signs." The Exodus signs and wonders that Jeremiah's prayer recalls are the framework within which the NT presents Christ's miracles as the new-Exodus signs of the messianic age. Jesus's miracles are not isolated marvels but signs of the new Exodus.
21: The specific recollection — you brought your people Israel out of Egypt with signs and wonders, with a strong hand and outstretched arm, and with great terror — is the creedal Exodus formula (Deuteronomy 26:8). Hebrews 3:1-6 presents Jesus as greater than Moses, who led the first Exodus — Jesus is the Son over the house that Moses only served in. The outstretched arm that brought Israel out of Egypt is the same power that raised Jesus from the dead (Ephesians 1:19-20). The Exodus formula is christologically reappropriated across the NT.
22: The inheritance given — you gave them this land that you swore to their fathers to give them, a land flowing with milk and honey — is the covenant promise fulfilled in the land. Hebrews 4:8-11 interprets the land-rest typologically: Joshua gave the physical rest, but the Sabbath rest that remains for the people of God is the spiritual rest Christ provides. The "land flowing with milk and honey" is a type of the "better country" (Hebrews 11:16) that the patriarchs sought and which is fulfilled in the heavenly city whose builder and maker is God.
23: The contrast — they entered and took possession of it, but they did not obey your voice or walk in your law — establishes the tragic gap between possession of the gift and obedience to the giver. Paul's analysis of Israel in Romans 9-11 engages exactly this paradox: Israel received the adoption, the glory, the covenants, the law, the worship, the promises, and the patriarchs — yet the majority did not believe (Romans 9:4-7). The failure of covenant obedience despite covenant gift establishes the need for the new covenant, where obedience is not demanded from without but written within by the Spirit.
24: The report of the siege mounds — the siege mounds have come up to the city to take it, and the city is given into the hands of the Chaldeans — while Jeremiah prays, the catastrophe is unfolding in real time. The prayer offered in the midst of visible disaster is the pattern of NT faith: Paul praying in prison (Philippians 4:6-7); the disciples praying during Herod's persecution (Acts 12:5); Revelation's prayers of the saints ascending as incense even as judgment unfolds (Revelation 8:3-4). Faith and prayer are not alternatives to facing reality but the posture in which reality is faced.
25: Jeremiah's perplexity — yet you, O Lord YHWH, have said to me: Buy the field for money and get witnesses — though the city is given into the hands of the Chaldeans — is the honest naming of the apparent absurdity: God commanded the purchase of land that was about to be lost. This is the structure of faith described in Hebrews 11:1: "the substance of things hoped for, the evidence of things not seen." The purchase of the apparently worthless field is the enacted parable of what the NT calls faith — acting on the divine word in defiance of visible evidence.
26: YHWH's response beginning with the divine self-identification — the word of YHWH came to Jeremiah: Behold, I am YHWH, the God of all flesh — reasserts divine sovereignty over all of human life. The claim "God of all flesh" (ʾElohei kol-basar) is the foundation for the universal scope of the new covenant. Paul argues in Romans 3:29-30: "Is God the God of Jews only? Is he not the God of Gentiles also? Yes, of Gentiles also, since God is one." The God who is God of all flesh is the God whose new covenant, through Christ, is for all nations.
27: Is anything too hard for me? — the divine rhetorical question echoing 17b and Genesis 18:14, here applied directly to the restoration promise. Matthew 19:26: "With man this is impossible, but with God all things are possible." The resurrection is the definitive answer to this question: what was impossible for human hope — the reversal of death — YHWH accomplished in Christ. Every instance of "is anything too hard for me?" in the OT is answered finally and permanently by Easter morning.
28: The oracle — I will give this city into the hands of the Chaldeans and Nebuchadnezzar — is the confirmation that the judgment is real and will be executed. The judgment that Jeremiah announces is not softened by the purchase of the field; both truths coexist: judgment is coming and restoration is coming. The NT holds the same tension: Romans 11:22 ("note then the kindness and the severity of God") — the cross simultaneously enacts the severity of judgment and the kindness of redemption. Both are real; neither cancels the other.
29: The burning of the houses on whose rooftops offerings were made to Baal and drink offerings were poured out to other gods, to provoke me to anger — the specific covenant violations that brought the specific covenant curse of fire. The NT uses fire as the image of judgment on false worship: Revelation 17-18 describes the burning of Babylon, the city of false religion. The judgment of fire on the rooftop altars of Baal is the historical type of the eschatological judgment on all idolatrous systems. Christ's return brings the definitive burning of all that is opposed to the living God.
30: The summary of accumulated sin — the people of Israel and Judah have done nothing but evil in my sight from their youth — is the verdict of universal covenant failure. This matches Paul's summary in Romans 3:10-18: "there is no one righteous, not even one... all have turned aside." The comprehensive failure of Israel and Judah is not exceptional but representative — the corruption of the covenant people reflects the universal human condition. This establishes the scope of what Christ's redemption must address: not a minor reform but a comprehensive new creation.
31: The specific enumeration — this city has aroused my anger and wrath, from the day it was built to this day, so that I will remove it from my sight — is the accumulated weight of covenant violation from the very beginning. The "from the day it was built" mirrors the "from your youth" of v30: the problem is congenital, not episodic. The NT diagnosis is the same: the law brought knowledge of sin but not its cure (Romans 3:20; Galatians 3:10). The deep-rootedness of the problem is what makes the new covenant's radical solution (new heart, indwelling Spirit) necessary rather than merely sufficient.
32: The catalog of covenant violation — the kings and officials, the priests and prophets, the men of Judah and the inhabitants of Jerusalem — encompasses all social classes and offices. No one is exempt from the indictment; every stratum of society participated in the covenant failure. Romans 3:23 states the same universality: "for all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God." The comprehensive scope of the accusation matches the comprehensive scope of the solution: "all who believe" (Romans 3:22), "all nations" (Matthew 28:19), "every tongue" (Philippians 2:11).
33: The people turning their back to me and not their face; I have taught them persistently but they have not listened — the divine teacher refused by the student. Jesus laments the same: "O Jerusalem, Jerusalem, the city that kills the prophets... how often would I have gathered your children together as a hen gathers her brood under her wings, and you were not willing!" (Matthew 23:37). The unwillingness to receive divine instruction is the core problem, and the new covenant's solution — law written on the heart — is precisely its solution: those who have been given a new heart will not turn their backs.
34: The high places of Baal in the Valley of Hinnom, to offer their sons and daughters to Molech — the child-sacrifice that was the extreme form of covenant violation. The Valley of Hinnom (Ge-hinnom, Gehenna) becomes in the NT the image for eschatological judgment (Mark 9:43-48; Matthew 5:22). Jesus's use of Gehenna to describe the eschatological fire draws on this location where children were burned as offerings — the ultimate perversion of covenant worship. The place of covenant horror becomes the image of eschatological judgment.
35: YHWH's declaration that I did not command them, nor did it enter my mind, that they should do this abomination — the explicit repudiation of child sacrifice as having any divine sanction. The NT fulfillment inverts this: God does give his own Son, not as a burnt offering forced by foreign religion, but as the willing self-sacrifice of love (John 3:16; Romans 8:32). What the pagan nations did to their children by compulsion and with divine repudiation, the Father does with his Son willingly and lovingly — the difference being infinite, as love differs from murder.
36: The transition from judgment to restoration — now therefore, thus says the Lord, the God of Israel, concerning this city of which you say: it is given into the hand of the king of Babylon through sword, famine, and pestilence — the "now therefore" pivot that turns from accusation to promise. The rhetorical pivot from "you have sinned" to "here is my promise" is the structure of the gospel: "but God demonstrates his love for us in that while we were still sinners, Christ died for us" (Romans 5:8). The gospel's "but God" is the NT version of Jeremiah's "now therefore."
37: The promise — I will gather them from all the lands to which I drove them in my anger and great wrath, and I will bring them back to this place and let them dwell in safety — is the diaspora-gathering promise. The NT fulfillment is Pentecost (Acts 2:5-11 — Jews from every nation gathered in Jerusalem) and the worldwide mission gathering from every nation into the one body of Christ. Ephesians 2:14-18: Christ "has made us both one and has broken down in his flesh the dividing wall of hostility... creating in himself one new man."
38: The new covenant formula — they shall be my people and I will be their God; I will give them one heart and one way, that they may fear me forever — combines the covenant-formula ("my people/their God") with the new-covenant element of internal unity ("one heart and one way"). Philippians 2:2: "complete my joy by being of the same mind, having the same love, being in full accord and of one mind." The unity of heart that Jeremiah promises is the unity of the community in Christ — achieved not by external pressure but by the Spirit's unifying work in those who share one Lord, one faith, one baptism (Ephesians 4:3-6).
39: The promise that I will put the fear of me in their hearts, that they may not turn from me — the internalized reverence that produces permanent fidelity, replacing the externally commanded but internally unfelt covenant obedience. Romans 8:15: "you did not receive the spirit of slavery to fall back into fear, but you have received the Spirit of adoption." The "fear" that the new covenant instills is not slavish terror but the reverent love of an adopted child — the Spirit of sonship who produces the genuine devotion that the old covenant demanded but could not produce.
40: A direct type: I will make with them an everlasting covenant (berit ʿolam), that I will not turn away from doing good to them; I will put the fear of me in their hearts, that they may not turn from me. Hebrews 13:20-21 applies the "everlasting covenant" to Christ's resurrection: "the God of peace who brought again from the dead our Lord Jesus Christ, by the blood of the eternal covenant, equip you with everything good that you may do his will." The "everlasting covenant" of Jeremiah 32 is identified as the covenant whose blood is Christ's, enacted by his resurrection. This is one of the clearest NT identifications of the new covenant with the person and work of Christ.
41: The joy of restoration — I will rejoice in doing them good, and I will plant them in this land in faithfulness, with all my heart and all my soul — is YHWH declaring that the restoration will not be reluctant or minimal but wholehearted. The divine "with all my heart and all my soul" is the measure of covenant commitment. Zephaniah 3:17 matches it: "YHWH your God is in your midst, a mighty one who will save; he will rejoice over you with gladness; he will quiet you by his love; he will exult over you with loud singing." In Christ, this divine joy over the restored covenant people is made flesh and manifested.
42: The assurance — as I have brought all this great disaster on this people, so I will bring on them all the good that I promise them — uses the certainty of the past disaster as the measure of the certainty of the future blessing. Paul applies the same logic: "he who did not spare his own Son but gave him up for us all — how will he not also with him graciously give us all things?" (Romans 8:32). The cross — the greatest disaster from one perspective — is the guarantee of the greatest blessing from another. The measure of God's severity becomes the measure of his grace.
43: The sign enacted — fields shall be bought in this land of which you are saying: it is a desolation, without man or beast; it is given into the hand of the Chaldeans — is the purchase carried out in defiance of the present reality. The structure of the sign-act is the structure of Christian baptism: the baptized person claims death and resurrection as their present reality in defiance of visible appearance. "You have died, and your life is hidden with Christ in God" (Colossians 3:3) — present claim, future visible vindication. The field purchase is faith-in-action against present evidence.
44: The comprehensive scope of the restoration — fields shall be bought for money, deeds signed and sealed and witnessed, in the land of Benjamin, in the places around Jerusalem, in the cities of Judah, the cities of the hill country, the cities of the lowland, and the cities of the Negeb — covers every region of the land. The final verse's geographical completeness is the picture of total restoration from which nothing is excluded. The NT counterpart is Revelation 21:5: "Behold, I am making all things new." The comprehensive restoration of every region of the land is a type of the comprehensive new creation that Christ accomplishes — where every dimension of the broken creation is restored, not just repaired at the edges.
